resources.string: Pick a starting point. Templates pre-fill recommended actions for common situations — you can adjust everything in the following steps.
Exercise
Raise target, reduce profile to 70%, disable SMB to prevent exercise lows.

During physical activity, muscles consume glucose faster and insulin sensitivity increases. Raising your temporary target beforehand gives the algorithm a head start — it will begin reducing insulin delivery early, building a buffer against exercise-induced lows. Disabling SMB is also recommended, since correction boluses during exercise can cause rapid drops. For best results, activate this scene 1–2 hours before you start exercising so the algorithm has time to lower your insulin on board.
Sick Day
Stronger profile (150%) to handle increased insulin resistance during illness.

Illness, fever, and stress hormones can dramatically increase insulin resistance — sometimes by 50% or more. A stronger profile (e.g. 150%) tells the algorithm to deliver more insulin across all calculations — basal rates, bolus suggestions, and correction factors. You can also add a temporary target or adjust the running mode in the following steps. Monitor closely and adjust as your body recovers.
Sick Day — Vomiting
Reduced profile with LGS — opposite of standard sick day when you cannot absorb food.

When you cannot keep food down, the standard sick day approach of increasing insulin can be dangerous. Vomiting means carbs are not being absorbed, so blood glucose may drop rather than rise. A reduced profile (60%) lowers insulin delivery, and LGS mode adds an extra safety net by suspending insulin if glucose falls. Disabling SMB prevents any extra boluses. Monitor frequently — check blood glucose every 1–2 hours and ketones every 2–4 hours. Seek medical help if vomiting persists more than a few hours or ketones rise above 1.5 mmol/L.
Sleep
Tighter target for better overnight control.

Overnight is when many people experience undetected highs or lows. A slightly tighter target during sleep lets the algorithm make finer corrections throughout the night. Pair this with a profile that reflects your nighttime basal needs for best results.
Pre-meal
Lower target to get ahead of the post-meal spike.

Setting a lower target 30–60 minutes before eating tells the algorithm to start building up insulin on board before carbs arrive. This helps blunt the post-meal spike that happens when food hits the bloodstream faster than insulin can act. Best used when you know a meal is coming soon.
Alcohol
LGS mode, reduced profile, SMB off — protects against delayed alcohol hypos.

Alcohol is one of the most dangerous situations for people with type 1 diabetes. The liver prioritizes processing alcohol over releasing glucose, which can cause delayed hypoglycemia 6–24 hours later — often overnight while you are asleep. LGS mode prevents insulin stacking by only reducing delivery when glucose drops. A reduced profile and disabled SMB further limit insulin on board. Always check your blood glucose before going to sleep and consider setting an alarm for a middle-of-the-night check.
Driving
Slightly higher target to stay safe behind the wheel.

Safe driving requires stable blood glucose above 5.0 mmol/L (90 mg/dL). In some countries, checking before and during long drives is a legal requirement. A slightly higher target gives the algorithm a safety buffer — it will work to keep you above the level where concentration and reaction time start to decline. If you go low while driving, pull over immediately, treat the hypo, and wait at least 45 minutes after glucose returns to normal before continuing.
Swimming
Disconnect pump for swimming, showering, or water activities.

When you disconnect your pump, insulin delivery stops completely — no basal, no boluses, no corrections. The algorithm switches to disconnected mode and stops making adjustments. This is normal for short periods like showering or swimming. When the scene ends, the pump reconnects and the algorithm resumes automatically. Keep disconnects short — without basal insulin, blood glucose will start rising within 1–2 hours.
Luteal Phase
Increased profile to handle pre-period insulin resistance.

Many women experience increased insulin resistance during the luteal phase (roughly 1–2 weeks before their period). This is caused by rising progesterone levels that reduce insulin sensitivity. The effect varies enormously between individuals — some notice a 10–30% increase in insulin needs, others see minimal change. Start with a conservative increase (115%) and adjust based on your own patterns over several cycles. Tracking this in CarePortal helps identify your personal rhythm.
Hot Weather
Reduced profile — heat accelerates insulin absorption.

High temperatures cause blood vessels to dilate, which accelerates insulin absorption — the same dose hits faster and harder. This can lead to unexpected lows, especially during outdoor activities. A modest profile reduction (85%) compensates for the faster absorption. Stay hydrated, monitor more frequently, and be aware that insulin stored in a warm pump or pen may degrade. If you are also exercising in the heat, consider combining this with the exercise scene for additional protection.
Medical Procedure
Open loop with SMB off for surgery or medical procedures.

During surgery or medical procedures, anesthesia and stress hormones make blood glucose unpredictable. Open loop mode is recommended because the algorithm cannot reliably predict what your body will do under these conditions. A higher target (150 mg/dL) provides a safety margin against intraoperative hypoglycemia. Disabling SMB prevents unexpected insulin delivery during the procedure. Always inform medical staff that you are wearing an insulin pump and CGM. For procedures longer than 3 hours, your team may switch to intravenous insulin instead.
Blank
Start from scratch with no pre-selected actions.

A profile switch changes your basal rates, insulin sensitivity factor (ISF), and insulin-to-carb ratios (IC) all at once. You can scale everything by percentage — for example, 110% increases basal delivery, bolus calculations, and carb absorption rates by 10%. You can also switch to a different profile entirely if you have one set up for specific situations.

A temporary target tells the algorithm to aim for a different blood glucose level for a set period. With a higher target, the algorithm reacts more slowly to rising glucose and targets a higher value overall — this reduces insulin delivery and helps prevent lows during exercise. With a lower target, the algorithm responds sooner and delivers more insulin — useful for getting ahead of a meal. Pick one of your presets below — the target value and reason are already configured there.

Super Micro Boluses (SMB) let the algorithm deliver insulin upfront as small boluses while temporarily stopping basal delivery to compensate — effectively borrowing insulin from the future for a faster response. Disabling SMB prevents this and limits the algorithm to adjusting basal rates only. The response becomes slower and more gradual, which is often preferred during exercise or sleep.

The running mode determines how much autonomy the algorithm has. In Closed Loop, it adjusts insulin delivery automatically. LGS (Low Glucose Suspend) only reduces or stops insulin when glucose is falling toward a low — it will not increase delivery. Open Loop calculates suggestions but waits for you to confirm each one.

A CarePortal event is a log entry — it does not change insulin delivery or algorithm behavior. Use it to mark activities, situations, or notes in your treatment timeline. You can review these records in Treatments - CarePortal and in Nightscout, helping you and your doctor spot patterns in your data.

Duration controls how long this scene stays active. When time runs out, duration-based actions (temporary target, profile switch, running mode) expire on their own, while persistent changes (like the SMB toggle) are automatically reverted to their previous values. Setting duration to 0 makes the scene indefinite — all actions stay active until you end the scene manually.
Choose a name and icon that make this scene easy to recognize at a glance. You will see these on the activation button and in the active scene banner on your overview screen.
Include %1$s in scene
Finish
